title: Quasi-Linear Size PCPs with Small Soundness from High-Dimensional Expanders
speaker: Mitali Bafna
abstract:
The theory of probabilistically checkable proofs (PCPs) shows how to encode a proof for any theorem into a format where the theorem's correctness can be verified by making only a constant number of queries to the proof. The PCP Theorem [ALMSS] is a fundamental result in computer science with far-reaching consequences in hardness of approximation, cryptography, and cloud computing. A PCP has two important parameters: 1) the size of the encoding, and 2) soundness, which is the probability that the verifier accepts an incorrect proof, both of which we wish to minimize.
In 2005, Dinur gave a surprisingly elementary and purely combinatorial proof of the PCP theorem that relies only on tools such as graph expansion, while also giving the first construction of 2-query PCPs with quasi-linear size and constant soundness (close to 1). Our work improves upon Dinur's PCP and constructs 2-query, quasi-linear size PCPs with arbitrarily small constant soundness. As a direct consequence, assuming the exponential time hypothesis, we get that no approximation algorithm for 3-SAT can achieve an approximation ratio significantly better than 7/8 in time 2^{n/polylog n}. 
In this talk, I will introduce PCPs and discuss the components that go into our proof. This talk is based on joint work with Dor Minzer and Nikhil Vyas, with an appendix by Zhiwei Yun. Our work builds on the recent construction of linear-size direct product testers with small soundness by independent works of Bafna, Lifshitz and Minzer, as well as, Dikstein, Dinur and Lubotzky.

proof_stages.label: PCP-to-CSP framework and target theorem
proof_stages.summary: The talk recasts the PCP theorem as a gap-preserving reduction from 3SAT to two-query CSPs, identifies size and soundness as the key parameters, and states the new quasi-linear-size constant-alphabet PCP with arbitrary constant soundness and ETH hardness corollaries.

proof_stages.label: High-dimensional expanders as sparse hosts
proof_stages.summary: High-dimensional expanders are introduced as sparse graphs whose local neighborhoods and constant-size cliques retain expander-like connectivity, providing the sparse structured host graphs used later.

proof_stages.label: Direct-product gap amplification on sparse set systems
proof_stages.summary: Starting from a near-linear PCP with value bounded away from one, the speaker abstracts parallel repetition as a direct-product test, explains the dense blowup barrier, and invokes the theorem that HDX clique set systems have close-to-zero direct-product soundness precisely when they have coboundary expansion, with such sparse HDXs now constructible.

proof_stages.label: HDX-hosted amplification pipeline
proof_stages.summary: For CSPs whose constraints already lie on the HDX one-skeleton, a clique-based two-query CSP and the HDX direct-product tester amplify value close to zero with constant blowup, yielding the overall plan: embed an arbitrary CSP on an HDX, amplify, and then use query composition to restore constant alphabet size.

proof_stages.label: Ordinary routing embedding and its soundness loss
proof_stages.summary: The first arrow is compared with prior routing embeddings that decompose a CSP graph into matchings and route each matching through a fixed host graph, but single-path routing causes a soundness loss proportional to the path length and greatly enlarges the alphabet.

proof_stages.label: Fault-tolerant routing abstraction
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines efficient edge-fault-tolerant routing protocols and states the general reduction theorem that any fixed graph supporting such protocols can host arbitrary CSPs while preserving soundness up to the desired parameters.

proof_stages.label: Edge-fault-tolerant routing on HDXs
proof_stages.summary: Using ideas from distributed computing but strengthening them from vertex faults to arbitrary edge faults, sufficiently good polylog-degree HDXs with many cliques and links are shown to support the required routing protocols with polylogarithmic work per vertex.

proof_stages.label: Parameter compatibility and wrap-up
proof_stages.summary: The final parameter discussion explains that the same constructed HDX can satisfy both the direct-product/coboundary-expansion requirements and the routing requirements, completing the reduction, and closes with alternative amplification, open size-efficiency questions, and related algorithmic or distributed-computing implications.
